14: A type: 'So he went down and dipped himself seven times in the Jordan, according to the word of the man of God, and his flesh was restored like the flesh of a little child, and he was clean.' Naaman's healing is one of the OT's most theologically rich type-narratives: (1) the outsider (a Gentile military commander) receives healing denied to insiders (Israelite lepers, Luke 4:27); (2) the healing comes through water — specifically the Jordan, the covenant-crossing water; (3) the means is foolishly simple (wash seven times) — Naaman expects a dramatic prophetic ritual; (4) the result is total cleansing. Jesus's citation of Naaman in Luke 4:27 to explain his own ministry to Nazareth (grace going to unexpected outsiders) and the Baptist's baptism in the Jordan both stand in the Naaman-and-the-Jordan typological stream: the Jordan is the water of covenant-entry and cleansing, fulfilled in baptism into Christ.
1: Naaman is introduced with maximum social prestige: commander of the Aramean army, great man (gāḏôl), highly regarded (nᵉśûʾ pānîm — literally “of lifted face” — honored by his master), victorious in battle — YHWH himself is said to have given him the victories. And then: mᵉṣōrāʿ — he was a leper. The structure is the ancient rhetorical form for tragic irony: greatness exhaustively catalogued, then undercut by a single condition that all the greatness cannot address. Wealth, honor, power, military success — and leprosy. Luke 4:27: Jesus cites Naaman specifically as the paradigm case of grace going to unexpected outsiders. The commander of Israel’s enemy army becomes the first recorded Gentile recipient of covenantal healing.
2: A little girl from Israel — captured in an Aramean raid, serving Naaman’s wife — carries the word that will heal him. She is unnamed, enslaved, from the enemy people, and a child. The concentric circles of marginalization are total. Yet she is the person through whom the redemptive word travels. 1 Corinthians 1:27-29: “God chose what is foolish in the world to shame the wise; God chose what is weak in the world to shame the strong; God chose what is low and despised in the world.” The word that heals Naaman comes from the lowest person in his household.
3: “If only my lord were before the prophet who is in Samaria! He would cure him of his leprosy.” The girl’s testimony is confident and specific: the prophet who is in Samaria can heal. She does not say “I think,” or “perhaps.” Her faith in Elisha’s capacity is unqualified. Acts 8:4: “those who were scattered went about preaching the word” — the captives of the dispersion become the missionaries to the Gentiles. The Samaritan woman at the well (John 4:39: “many Samaritans believed because of the woman’s testimony”) is the NT version of the same pattern: a woman’s witness brings outsiders to the one who heals.
4: Naaman reports the girl’s word to the king of Aram — from slave girl to king in one step. The redemptive word travels upward through the social hierarchy by the force of its content, not the status of its carrier. The king of Aram takes the girl’s testimony seriously enough to dispatch a diplomatic mission. Even power listens when desperation is great enough.
5: The king sends Naaman with ten talents of silver, six thousand shekels of gold, and ten changes of clothing — enormous wealth intended to purchase the healing. The gift-package attempts to commodify what is freely given. Acts 8:18-19: Simon Magus offers money for the gift of the Spirit. The pattern of those who approach grace as a transaction is consistent: they assume divine favor can be obtained by what is brought rather than by what is received. The wealth Naaman carries will be irrelevant to his healing and a temptation to Gehazi (v20-27).
6: The king of Aram writes to the king of Israel: “I have sent my servant Naaman to you, that you may cure him of his leprosy.” The diplomatic channel bypasses the prophet and addresses the king — the institutional approach to grace. The king of Israel is not the healing agent; the prophet is. Approaching God through official channels (political, institutional, hereditary) instead of through the one God has appointed is a recurring human error. John 14:6: “No one comes to the Father except through me.”
7: The king of Israel tears his clothes: “Am I God, to give death and life, that this man sends to me to cure a man of his leprosy?” The king’s question is theologically correct — only God can heal leprosy — but practically wrong, because the prophet who speaks for that God is in his kingdom. His tearing of garments misreads the diplomatic letter as a pretext for war; he cannot imagine that YHWH could heal through his prophet. The institutional leader who cannot see the prophetic resource in his own land.
8: Elisha hears of the king’s distress and sends word: “Let him come to me, that he may know there is a prophet in Israel.” The purpose of the healing is stated explicitly: wəyēḏaʿ kî yēš nāḇîʾ bəyiśrāʾēl — the knowledge of YHWH’s presence in Israel through his prophet. John 20:31: “these are written so that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God.” The miracles are not ends in themselves but revelation — they create knowledge of who YHWH is and where he acts.
9: Naaman comes with his horses and chariots — the full military display of a great man — and stands at the door of Elisha’s house. The power and pomp of the Aramean general before the threshold of the prophet’s ordinary house: the great man waiting at the ordinary door is the reversal of expected social power. Jesus: “Behold, I stand at the door and knock” (Revelation 3:20) — the divine one waiting at the human door is the ultimate reversal.
10: Elisha does not come out but sends a messenger: “Go and wash in the Jordan seven times, and your flesh will be restored, and you shall be clean.” The instruction has three qualities that give offense: (1) Elisha does not come in person; (2) the instruction is insultingly simple; (3) the Jordan — an inferior Israelite river — is specified. The simplicity of the gospel has always been its stumbling block. The means of salvation — faith in the crucified, baptism into his death — appears foolish and inadequate to those who expect something commensurate with their need.
11: Naaman is angry: “I expected him to come out to me in person, call on the name of the LORD his God, wave his hand over the place, and cure me of my leprosy.” Naaman has a detailed script for how the healing should work: the prophet appears, invokes YHWH, performs a dramatic gesture. What he expects is a theurgic ritual — the manipulation of divine power by a qualified specialist using proper technique. 1 Corinthians 1:22-23: “Jews demand signs and Greeks seek wisdom, but we preach Christ crucified.” The cross is foolishness because it refuses the script of power and wisdom; Naaman’s rage at the Jordan instruction is the Gentile form of the same offense.
12: “Are not Abana and Pharpar, the rivers of Damascus, better than all the waters of Israel? Could I not wash in them and be clean?” The question is perfectly logical by every natural standard: the Abana and Pharpar (Barada and Awaj rivers) are indeed clear, full rivers; the Jordan is murkier and less impressive. But the healing is not in the water’s quality — it is in the word attached to it. The sacramental principle: the efficacy of baptism is not in the water’s chemistry but in the Word of God that commands and promises. Luther: “it is not the water that does these things, but the Word of God which is in and with the water.”
13: His servants appeal: “My father (ʾāḇî), if the prophet had told you to do something great, would you not have done it? How much more, when he says to you, Wash and be clean?” The servants’ logic is the logic of the lesser to the greater. Naaman would have performed any dramatic, costly requirement — why refuse the simple one? The pride that can bear the great sacrifice but cannot bear the humble act is the characteristic human resistance to grace. John 13:8-9: Peter refuses to have his feet washed, then asks for a full bath — the overcorrection of pride brought low.
15: “I know that there is no God in all the earth except in Israel.” The Gentile confession after healing: the definitive monotheistic acknowledgment from a pagan military commander who came to buy a healing and instead received a revelation. The structure of Naaman’s conversion — encounter with a captive witness (v3), dramatic encounter with the prophet, healing through simple obedience, personal confession — is the pattern of Gentile conversion in Acts (cf. Acts 10:34-48: Cornelius; Acts 16:27-34: Philippian jailer). Ephesians 2:12-13: those who were “strangers to the covenants of promise, having no hope and without God in the world” are “brought near by the blood of Christ.”
16: “As the LORD lives, before whom I stand, I will receive nothing.” Elisha refuses all payment for the healing. The gift of healing that cannot be purchased is the grace that must remain free. Matthew 10:8: “You received without paying; give without pay.” The refusal of payment is itself a proclamation: YHWH’s gifts are not for sale. Elisha’s refusal contrasts with every commercial religious system then and now; it anticipates Jesus’s temple cleansing (Matthew 21:12-13) — the house of prayer cannot be a house of commerce.
17: Naaman requests two mule-loads of Israelite earth to build an altar for YHWH at Damascus — he will worship YHWH only on Israelite soil. The earth-theology is transitional: in the ANE, gods were considered to have territorial jurisdiction; Naaman imports the territory of YHWH’s land. The request is simultaneously theologically limited (YHWH still geographically bound in Naaman’s mind) and theologically revolutionary (a Gentile worshiping YHWH in his own country). John 4:21-24: Jesus dismantles the territorial theology entirely — “the hour is coming when you will worship the Father neither on this mountain nor in Jerusalem... true worshipers will worship the Father in spirit and truth.” Naaman’s two mule-loads of earth point toward the worship that no longer needs any particular soil.
18: “When my master goes into the house of Rimmon to worship there, leaning on my arm, and I bow myself in the house of Rimmon — when I bow myself in the house of Rimmon, may YHWH pardon your servant in this one matter.” The Rimmon problem is the first missionary pastoral dilemma: the Gentile convert who must function in a context of unavoidable idolatry. Naaman is not asking permission to worship Rimmon; he is asking pardon for an unavoidable bodily participation in a ceremony he will not internally affirm. Elisha’s “go in peace” without qualification is remarkable pastoral accommodation. The Apostolic Council (Acts 15) wrestles with the same question: what does the Gentile convert owe to the full Torah? The answer in both cases: not the full burden, but the core of covenantal faith.
19: “Go in peace.” — lēk lᵉšālôm. The word of release given to the healed Gentile. The same word that ends Elijah’s encounter with the widow (1 Kings 17:23: “See, your son lives”) and will be spoken by Jesus at departures of healing (“Your faith has saved you; go in peace,” Luke 7:50; 2 Kings 8:48). The šālôm word is the blessing that accompanies those whom YHWH has restored and released.
20: Gehazi’s interior monologue: “My master has let this Aramean off too lightly by not accepting from him what he brought.” The resentment of grace extended to the outsider — the elder son’s reaction when the father throws a feast for the prodigal (Luke 15:28-30: “I have served you these many years, and you never gave me a young goat... but when this son of yours came, who has devoured your property with prostitutes, you killed the fattened calf for him”). Gehazi’s “too lightly” is the disciple’s refusal of the logic of grace: that the gift should be given freely, without extraction.
21: Gehazi runs after Naaman. When Naaman sees him coming, he steps down from his chariot to meet him — the just-healed Gentile showing honor to the prophet’s servant. The newly converted Naaman is more gracious in his encounter with the greedy servant than the servant is in his exploitation of the convert. The convert who was the recipient of grace acts with the generosity the disciple lacks.
22: Gehazi lies to Naaman: “My master has sent me” — invoking Elisha’s authority he does not have, for a need he has invented. The lie compounds the greed with false representation of the prophet. Judas: “What will you give me if I deliver him to you?” (Matthew 26:15) — the disciple who monetizes his access to the master. The pattern of the inner-circle disciple whose proximity to the grace-giver becomes an occasion for exploitation.
23: Naaman gives generously — two talents of silver and two changes of clothing, more than Gehazi asked. The healed man’s generosity contrasts with the servant’s scheme. Naaman, the recent convert, gives from gratitude; Gehazi, the long-serving insider, takes from greed. The inversion of the expected generosity-calculus.
24: Gehazi takes the gifts to the hill, stores them in the house, and dismisses the men who carried them. The concealment — putting the goods away before returning to Elisha — is the action of one who knows the act will not bear scrutiny. Achan after the Jericho ban (Joshua 7:21: “I saw among the spoil... I coveted them and took them... they are hidden in the earth inside my tent”) — the pattern of the covetous theft hidden from the community.
25: Gehazi returns and stands before Elisha as if nothing has happened. “Your servant has not gone anywhere.” The lie direct — the denial of a specific action to the one who already knows. Peter before the servant girl (Mark 14:68: “I neither know nor understand what you mean”); Ananias and Sapphira before Peter (Acts 5:3-4: “you have not lied to men but to God”). The lie told to the prophet is the lie told to YHWH himself.
26: “Did not my heart go with you when the man stepped down from his chariot to meet you?” — Elisha’s prophetic knowledge encompasses what Gehazi thought was private. Then the rebuke: “Is this a time to receive money and garments, olive orchards and vineyards, sheep and oxen, male servants and female servants?” The expanding list of things that silver can buy — the catalogue of covetousness — shows how the first acquisition opens the imagination to all that follows. 1 Timothy 6:10: “the love of money is a root of all kinds of evil.” Gehazi’s single act of greed opens the door to a life organized around acquisition.
27: “Therefore the leprosy of Naaman shall cling to you and to your descendants forever.” Gehazi goes out white as snow with leprosy — the disease that left Naaman transfers to the servant who profited from his healing. The judgment is exact: Gehazi exploited the grace given to a leper; now he receives the condition from which that grace had freed the Gentile. The leprosy “forever” (lᵉʿôlām) is the permanent consequence of the permanent covenant violation. The Judas parallel: “It would have been better for that man if he had not been born” (Matthew 26:24) — the insider who exploits the master’s gift inherits a judgment proportional to the privilege he betrayed.
